examples.title: Basic mkdir Operations
examples.description: Common mkdir command examples for file system organization
examples.code:
# Create a single directory
mkdir myfolder

# Create multiple directories
mkdir dir1 dir2 dir3

# Create nested directories (with parent creation)
mkdir -p projects/webapp/frontend

# Create directory with specific permissions
mkdir -m 755 shared_folder

# Create private directory (only owner access)
mkdir -m 700 private_folder

# Verbose output (shows what is created)
mkdir -v important_docs

# Combined options
mkdir -pv -m 755 backup/2024/data
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the most common uses of mkdir including basic creation, nested directories, permission setting, and verbose output.

examples.title: Creating Project Directory Structure
examples.description: Example showing how to set up a complete project structure with one command
examples.code:
# Create a typical web project structure
mkdir -p myproject/{src/{css,js,components},tests,docs,config}

# This creates:
# myproject/
# ├── src/
# │ ├── css/
# │ ├── js/
# │ └── components/
# ├── tests/
# ├── docs/
# └── config/
examples.explanation: Using brace expansion with mkdir -p creates an entire directory tree in one command, very useful for setting up project structures.

examples.title: Error Handling with mkdir
examples.description: Understanding mkdir exit codes and error conditions
examples.code:
# Check exit status
mkdir existing_dir
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
 echo "Directory created successfully"
else
 echo "Failed to create directory"
fi

# Safe directory creation (skip if exists)
mkdir -p myfolder

# Create with error checking
mkdir -m 755 newfolder 2>/dev/null || echo "Creation failed"
examples.explanation: Shows how to handle errors, check exit codes, and use -p to safely handle existing directories.
